title: Ample liquidity for a safe and efficient banking system
summary: In a speech mostly devoted to the ample-reserves framework and the case for modernizing the operating target, Logan opens with a clear monetary-policy read: she would have preferred to hold rates steady at the late-October meeting, judging that the outlook did not call for a cut. She sees the labor market as roughly balanced and cooling slowly (unemployment 4.3 percent, break-even payrolls near 30,000) with risks tilted to the downside but manageable, while inflation remains too high and not convincingly on track to 2 percent. Having already mitigated downside risks with the September cut, she did not see a need to cut again and would find it difficult to cut in December absent clear evidence of faster disinflation or sharper labor cooling. The bulk of the remarks defend ending asset runoff on Dec. 1 and the efficiency of ample, interest-bearing reserves.

key_quotes.quote: I would have preferred to hold interest rates steady at this week’s FOMC meeting.
key_quotes.context: Her stance on the late-October rate cut.

key_quotes.quote: Inflation remains too high, taxing the budgets of businesses and families, and appears likely to exceed the FOMC’s 2 percent target for too much longer. This economic outlook didn’t call for cutting rates.
key_quotes.context: Price-stability concern as the reason against the cut.

key_quotes.quote: The labor market remains roughly balanced. At 4.3 percent, the latest reading on the unemployment rate was up only slightly over the past year on net.
And I’d find it difficult to cut rates again in December unless there is clear evidence that inflation will fall faster than expected or that the labor market will cool more rapidly.
